step_by_step_thinking:
Clinical reasoning is important here to understand why one option might be more correct than others. The maxilla continues growing until approximately age 20, making it distinct from other skull bones that stop growth earlier or later but not as late as this.

My Answer: A To further refine my response based on clinical knowledge:

The maxillary sinuses begin developing before birth and grow significantly during childhood reaching near-adult size by around ages 8-9 years with continued minor development up to about age 25. This prolonged period of growth makes them unique among craniofacial structures including paranasal sinuses when compared to ethmoidals (peaking adolescence), frontales (largely developed by puberty), and sphenoids which also complete growth much sooner typically ending their expansion process well before young adult hood.

This prolonged period of growth makes them unique among craniofacial structures including paranasal sinuses when compared to ethmoidals (peaking adolescence), frontales (largely developed by puberty), and sphenoids which also complete growth much sooner typically ending their expansion process well before young adult hood. 

Answer: A Maxillary Sinus
